nameMeanings.name: Fowke
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of Family Names in Britain and Ireland © University of the West of England 2016
meanings: Norman, English: relationship name from the Middle English, Anglo-Norman French personal name Folk, Fulk, Fuke. Compare Foxon . For a variant form of the personal name see Foulkes . See also Fudge , which may sometimes be a variant form.

variants.name: Fudge
meanings: English (of Norman origin): from the Middle English, Anglo-Norman French personal name Fulch or Fuche, usually a pet form of Old French Fulcher, Fucher (see Fulcher ). Voicing of ‘ch’ to soft ‘g’ gave rise to Fudge. The name may sometimes have been interchangeable with Fowke (for which see Foulk 1), derived from a pet form of this and other Old French personal names in Fulc-.
English: habitational name from Fuge, in Blackawton, Devon. This placename is composed of Old English feoh ‘cattle’ + wīc ‘farm, dairy farm, village’.

variants.name: Foulk
meanings: English (of Norman origin): from the Middle English and Anglo-Norman French personal name Fulk, which is of ancient Germanic origin, ultimately from an element meaning ‘folk, people’.

variants.name: Fogg
meanings: English (northern): possibly a variant of Fowke (see Foulk 1). It may derive from Middle English fogge ‘aftermath’ (i.e. grass left to grow after the hay has been cut, also applied to long grass in a water meadow), especially in northern England, where the term occurs as a specifying element in minor placenames, but as it is not found on its own as a simplex name, the derivation is doubtful without some supporting evidence. The word fogge, which is probably of Old Norse origin, is still in use as a dialect term in Craven, Yorkshire, and in eastern Lancashire. modern English fog ‘thick mist’ is first attested in the 16th century and is unlikely to be the source of the surname.

variants.name: Fuke
meanings: Norman, English:  see  Fowke   and  compare  Fook  .

variants.name: Folk
meanings: German: variant of   Volk   1 and, in North America, also an altered form of this. This form of the surname is also found in Czechia.

variants.name: Focke
meanings: North German, East Frisian, and Dutch: from the ancient Germanic personal name Folco, a pet form of various names beginning with Folc- or Volk-; cognate with Foulk 1.
English (of Norman origin): variant of   Foulk   1. See also   Foulks  .

variants.name: Foulke
meanings: English (Derbyshire and Shropshire): variant of   Foulk  .

variants.name: Fulk
meanings: Altered form of German   Volk   or   Falk  .

nameMeanings.name: Richard
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rikert
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

historicEvents.title: Wycliffe's Bible
historicEvents.description: John Wycliffe, English theologian and professor at the University of Oxford, completed a translation of the Bible from the Latin Vulgate into Middle English. This translation is known as Wycliffe's Bible and was completed around 1382.

en.lifeSummary: Richard Fowke was born in 1380, in Staffordshire, England, United Kingdom as the son of Sir Edward Fowke and Juliana Fane. He had at least 1 son with Anne Allen. He died in 1403, in Staffordshire, England, at the age of 23.

fr.lifeSummary: Richard Fowke est né en 1380, à Staffordshire, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni, fils de Sir Edward Fowke et Juliana Fane. Il a eu au moins 1 fils avec Anne Allen. Il est décédé en 1403, à Staffordshire, Angleterre, à l’âge de 23 ans.

de.lifeSummary: Richard Fowke wurde 1380 in Staffordshire, England, United Kingdom, als Sohn von Sir Edward Fowke und Juliana Fane geboren. Er hatte mit Anne Allen mindestens 1 Sohn. Er starb 1403 im Alter von 23 Jahren in Staffordshire, England.

it.lifeSummary: Richard Fowke è nato il nel 1380 nel Staffordshire, Inghilterra, Regno Unito come figlio di Sir Edward Fowke e Juliana Fane. Lui avuto almeno 1 figlio con Anne Allen. Morì nel 1403, nel Staffordshire, England, all'età di 23 anni.

ja.lifeSummary: Richard Fowkeは1380年にイギリスイギリススタフォードシュアイアでSir Edward FowkeとJuliana Faneの息子として生まれた。彼はAnne Allenとの間に少なくとも1人の息子をもうけた。彼は1403年にイギリススタフォードシュアイアで23歳で死去した。

ko.lifeSummary: Richard Fowke(은)는 1380년 영국 잉글랜드 스태퍼드실르에서 Sir Edward Fowke(와)과 Juliana Fane의 아들로 태어났다. 그는 Anne Allen(와)과 적어도 한 명의 아들을 두었다. 그는 1403년 영국 스태퍼드실르에서 23세의 나이로 사망했다.

pt.lifeSummary: Richard Fowke nasceu em 1380, em Staffordshire, Inglaterra, United Kingdom filho de Sir Edward Fowke e Juliana Fane. Ele teve pelo menos 1 filho com Anne Allen. Ele faleceu em 1403, em Staffordshire, Inglaterra, com 23 anos.

ru.lifeSummary: Richard Fowke родился в 1380 г. в местности стаффордшир, Англия, Великобритания. Его родителей звали Sir Edward Fowke и Juliana Fane. У него был по крайней мере 1 сын от Anne Allen. Он умер в 1403 г. в местности стаффордшир, Англия в возрасте 23 лет.
